questions.question: What is Perlin noise?
options: Gradient-based smooth noise — places random gradient vectors at grid points, interpolates dot products. Natural-looking.
questions.correctAnswer: Gradient-based smooth noise — places random gradient vectors at grid points, interpolates dot products. Natural-looking.
questions.explanation: Ken Perlin 1983. Gradient at each lattice point. Dot with offset vector. Smoothstep interpolation. Zero at integer grid points.

o: Sum of multiple noise octaves at different scales: Σ amplitude·noise(frequency·x). Amplitude halves each octave, frequency doubles.
questions.a: Sum of multiple noise octaves at different scales: Σ amplitude·noise(frequency·x). Amplitude halves each octave, frequency doubles.
questions.e: Fractal Brownian Motion. Octaves=detail layers. Lacunarity=frequency multiplier. Gain=amplitude衰减. Terrain = 4-8 octaves.

o: Distance to nearest random feature point — creates cell-like patterns (stone, skin, organic)
questions.a: Distance to nearest random feature point — creates cell-like patterns (stone, skin, organic)
questions.e: Voronoi: random points in cells. F1 = nearest distance. F2-F1 = edge intensity. Produces cellular/crack patterns.

o: Apply noise to the INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns
questions.a: Apply noise to the INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns
questions.e: warp(x,y) = noise(x + noise(x+a,y+b), y + noise(x+c,y+d)). Distorted input → organic swirls. Used for smoke, clouds, lava.

questions.question: What is Poisson disc sampling?
options: Points placed with minimum distance r between all pairs, maximally dense. Natural-looking distribution.
questions.correctAnswer: Points placed with minimum distance r between all pairs, maximally dense. Natural-looking distribution.
questions.explanation: Dart-throwing: random candidates in annulus [r,2r]. Accept if no neighbor within r. Used for foliage placement, object scattering.

o: Point distribution with minimal low-frequency energy — no visible clumping. Optimal for rendering, dithering, scattering.
questions.a: Point distribution with minimal low-frequency energy — no visible clumping. Optimal for rendering, dithering, scattering.
questions.e: Blue noise spectrum: low energy at low frequencies (no clumping), peak at sampling frequency. Pre-computed textures (64×64, 128×128).

o: Divide domain into equal strata (grid cells), place one sample per stratum — guarantees coverage, reduces variance
questions.a: Divide domain into equal strata (grid cells), place one sample per stratum — guarantees coverage, reduces variance
questions.e: Stratified = jittered grid. One sample per cell, random position within. Guarantees no large gaps. Used for shadow maps, SSAO, TAA.

questions.question: What is an L-system?
options: A parallel string rewriting system — axiom + production rules → geometric interpretation via turtle graphics
questions.correctAnswer: A parallel string rewriting system — axiom + production rules → geometric interpretation via turtle graphics
questions.explanation: Lindenmayer 1968. Axiom→rule application→string→turtle graphics. F=draw, +=turn, []=push/pop for branching. Generates plants, trees.

o: Extracts isosurface from 3D scalar field — classifies 8 corners of each voxel, indexes 256-entry lookup table for triangles
questions.a: Extracts isosurface from 3D scalar field — classifies 8 corners of each voxel, indexes 256-entry lookup table for triangles
questions.e: Marching Cubes: for each voxel, 8 corners inside/outside → 256 configs → triangle vertices along edges. 15 unique topological cases.

questions.q: What is the scalar field input to Marching Cubes?
o: Any continuous function R³→R — distance field, noise, metaballs, implicit surfaces. Isosurface at f(x,y,z)=threshold.
questions.a: Any continuous function R³→R — distance field, noise, metaballs, implicit surfaces. Isosurface at f(x,y,z)=threshold.
questions.e: Marching Cubes doesn't care about the source. SDF = exact distance. Noise = organic terrain. Metaballs = blobby merging. All produce valid meshes.

questions.question: What is importance sampling?
options: Drawing samples from a distribution proportional to where the integrand is large — reduces variance
questions.correctAnswer: Drawing samples from a distribution proportional to where the integrand is large — reduces variance
questions.explanation: Instead of uniform sampling (wastes samples on low-contribution regions), sample where it matters. Essential for Monte Carlo rendering.

questions.q: What is the inversion method for sampling?
o: Compute CDF, invert it, sample uniform [0,1] → CDF⁻¹(uniform). Generates samples from arbitrary distribution.
questions.a: Compute CDF, invert it, sample uniform [0,1] → CDF⁻¹(uniform). Generates samples from arbitrary distribution.
questions.e: CDF = cumulative distribution. Invert analytically or numerically. Sample uniform→apply inverse→get sample from target distribution.

questions.question: What is an octree?
options: 3D spatial partitioning: recursively subdivide space into 8 octants. Each node has 0 or 8 children.
questions.correctAnswer: 3D spatial partitioning: recursively subdivide space into 8 octants. Each node has 0 or 8 children.
questions.explanation: Octree = 3D quadtree. Used for spatial indexing (collision, rendering), level-of-detail, voxel compression. UE uses for lightmass and some collision.

o: 3D pixels — volumetric elements. Store density, material, color per cell. Used for terrain, medical imaging, UE Voxel Plugin.
questions.a: 3D pixels — volumetric elements. Store density, material, color per cell. Used for terrain, medical imaging, UE Voxel Plugin.
questions.e: VOlumetric piXEL. Minecraft-style worlds, medical CT scans, UE Voxel Plugin. Marching Cubes converts voxels to meshes.

questions.question: What is the graphics pipeline from vertices to pixels?
options: Vertex shader → rasterizer → pixel shader → output merger. Programmable stages (shaders) + fixed-function (rasterizer).
questions.correctAnswer: Vertex shader → rasterizer → pixel shader → output merger. Programmable stages (shaders) + fixed-function (rasterizer).
questions.explanation: Vertex shader: transform vertices. Rasterizer: generate fragments. Pixel shader: compute color. Output merger: depth test, blend. UE's RHI abstracts this.

questions.q: What does fixed-function hardware do in the GPU pipeline?
o: Rasterizer (triangles→fragments), output merger (depth test, blend) — not programmable, just configurable
questions.a: Rasterizer (triangles→fragments), output merger (depth test, blend) — not programmable, just configurable
questions.e: Fixed-function = non-programmable stages. Rasterizer: generates fragments from triangles. Output merger: depth/stencil test, blending. Extremely fast dedicated silicon.
